"Fukushima Prefecture Official Ritual Shinhoyama Shrine for the Honoring of Spirits," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Fukushima Inari Shrine|ja|福島稲荷神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})*History of Fukushima City*, vol. 5, Fukushima City Board of Education, 1975, p. 790. * {{ill|Miyazaki Shrine|ja|宮崎神宮}} Museum of Antiquities ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists.*Record of the 2600th Anniversary Commemorative Ceremony*, vol. 7, 1940. * {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) * {{ill|Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine|ja|大宮氷川神社}} administrative office and envoy Saikan ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})Shinohara Naomichi, Watanabe Yōko, "Research on Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine: Sunami Takashi and the Kokui Architecture Research Institute," *Architectural History and Design*, 2016, pp. 359-360, Japanese Architectural Institute, 2016. * {{ill|Akita Gokoku Shrine|ja|秋田県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists. * {{ill|Toyotamahime Shrine (Minamikyūshū)|ja|豊玉姫神社 (南九州市)}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})*Chiran Local History*, Chiran Town, 1962. * {{ill|Inaba Shrine|ja|伊奈波神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}-{{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})*Summary of Inaba Shrine*, Inaba Shrine, 1941. * {{ill|Miyagi Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮城県護国神社}} ({{ill|1942|ja|1942年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid.*History of Miyagi Prefecture*, vol. 13 (Art and Architecture), Miyagi Prefecture History Publication Committee, 1980. * {{ill|Minatogawa Shrine|ja|湊川神社}} Gate ({{ill|1943|ja|1943年}})Kiyozō Kamiyama, "Gate of Minatogawa Shrine," *Travel and Tradition*, vol. 16, issue 7, 1943. * {{ill|Kagoshima Gokoku Shrine|ja|鹿児島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1948|ja|1948年}}) * {{ill|Sōjiji|ja|總持寺}} Great Ancestral Hall (with Tanaka Naitō) ({{ill|1962|ja|1962年}}) * {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社}} First Torii Gate (with Tanaka Naitō) ({{ill|1963|ja|1963年}})Research Records: Memorandum on Investigation into Relics of Former Shugendo Activities in Fukushima Prefecture (3) (http://www.anno-spero.org/renaissance/dentou/history/fukushima_3.html). === Publications === * "Architectural Techniques of Japan" (1937–1938), serialized in ''Tōyō Kenchiku''. * "Restoration of Nikkō Temples" (1957), published in ''Dainikkō''. == References == == Further Reading == * "Nihonmatsu Kōzō and Cultural Merit Award," ''Shūkyō {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} | practice_name = {{ill|Home Ministry (Japan)|ja|内務省}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|ja|伊奈波神社}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[1884]] [[March 26]] – [[1972]] [[September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[1908]], he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}}. By [[1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[National Treasures of Japan]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺}}. In [[1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}} in [[1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[World War II]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:Nawa Shrine - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|ja|1913年}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * {{ill|Kagoshima Shrine|ja|鹿児島神宮}} main shrine (unknown year) * {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ({{ill|1933|ja|1933年}}).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * {{ill|Nawa Shrine|ja|名和神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Hirakiki Shrine|ja|枚聞神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1936|ja|1936年}}) * {{ill|Fukushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|福島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Fukushima Prefecture Official Ritual Shinhoyama Shrine for the Honoring of Spirits," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Fukushima Inari Shrine|ja|福島稲荷神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})*History of Fukushima City*, vol. 5, Fukushima City Board of Education, 1975, p. 790. * {{ill|Miyazaki Shrine|ja|宮崎神宮}} Museum of Antiquities ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists.*Record of the 2600th Anniversary Commemorative Ceremony*, vol. 7, 1940. * {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) * {{ill|Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine|ja|大宮氷川神社}} administrative office and envoy Saikan ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})Shinohara Naomichi, Watanabe Yōko, "Research on Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine: Sunami Takashi and the Kokui Architecture Research Institute," *Architectural History and Design*, 2016, pp. 359-360, Japanese Architectural Institute, 2016. * {{ill|Akita Gokoku Shrine|ja|秋田県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists. * {{ill|Toyotamahime Shrine (Minamikyūshū)|ja|豊玉姫神社 (南九州市)}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})*Chiran Local History*, Chiran Town, 1962. * {{ill|Inaba Shrine|ja|伊奈波神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}-{{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})*Summary of Inaba Shrine*, Inaba Shrine, 1941. * {{ill|Miyagi Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮城県護国神社}} ({{ill|1942|ja|1942年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid.*History of Miyagi Prefecture*, vol. 13 (Art and Architecture), Miyagi Prefecture History Publication Committee, 1980. * {{ill|Minatogawa Shrine|ja|湊川神社}} Gate ({{ill|1943|ja|1943年}})Kiyozō Kamiyama, "Gate of {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} | practice_name = {{ill|Home Ministry (Japan)|ja|内務省}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|ja|伊奈波神社}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[1884]] [[March 26]] – [[1972]] [[September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[1908]], he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}}. By [[1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[National Treasures of Japan]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺}}. In [[1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}} in [[1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[World War II]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:Nawa Shrine - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|ja|1913年}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * {{ill|Kagoshima Shrine|ja|鹿児島神宮}} main shrine (unknown year) * {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ({{ill|1933|ja|1933年}}).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})Kōzō {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} | practice_name = {{ill|Home Ministry (Japan)|ja|内務省}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|ja|伊奈波神社}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[1884]] [[March 26]] – [[1972]] [[September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[1908]], he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}}. By [[1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[National Treasures of Japan]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺}}. In [[1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}} in [[1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[World War II]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:Nawa Shrine - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|ja|1913年}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * {{ill|Kagoshima Shrine|ja|鹿児島神宮}} main shrine (unknown year) * {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ({{ill|1933|ja|1933年}}).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * {{ill|Nawa Shrine|ja|名和神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Hirakiki Shrine|ja|枚聞神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1936|ja|1936年}}) * {{ill|Fukushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|福島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Fukushima Prefecture Official Ritual Shinhoyama Shrine for the Honoring of Spirits," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Fukushima Inari Shrine|ja|福島稲荷神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})*History of Fukushima City*, vol. 5, Fukushima City Board of Education, 1975, p. 790. * {{ill|Miyazaki Shrine|ja|宮崎神宮}} Museum of Antiquities ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists.*Record of the 2600th Anniversary Commemorative Ceremony*, vol. 7, 1940. * {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) * {{ill|Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine|ja|大宮氷川神社}} administrative office and envoy Saikan ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})Shinohara Naomichi, Watanabe Yōko, "Research on Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine: Sunami Takashi and the Kokui Architecture Research Institute," *Architectural History and Design*, 2016, pp. 359-360, Japanese Architectural Institute, 2016. * {{ill|Akita Gokoku Shrine|ja|秋田県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists. * {{ill|Toyotamahime Shrine (Minamikyūshū)|ja|豊玉姫神社 (南九州市)}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})*Chiran Local History*, Chiran Town, 1962. * {{ill|Inaba Shrine|ja|伊奈波神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}-{{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})*Summary of Inaba Shrine*, Inaba Shrine, 1941. * {{ill|Miyagi Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮城県護国神社}} ({{ill|1942|ja|1942年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid.*History of Miyagi Prefecture*, vol. 13 (Art and Architecture), Miyagi Prefecture History Publication Committee, 1980. * {{ill|Minatogawa Shrine|ja|湊川神社}} Gate ({{ill|1943|ja|1943年}})Kiyozō Kamiyama, "Gate of revision.text:
{{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} | practice_name = {{ill|Home Ministry (Japan)|ja|内務省}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|ja|伊奈波神社}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[1884]] [[March 26]] – [[1972]] [[September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[1908]], he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}}. By [[1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[National Treasures of Japan]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺}}. In [[1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}} in [[1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[World War II]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:Nawa Shrine - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|ja|1913年}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * {{ill|Kagoshima Shrine|ja|鹿児島神宮}} main shrine (unknown year) * {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ({{ill|1933|ja|1933年}}).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * {{ill|Nawa Shrine|ja|名和神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Hirakiki Shrine|ja|枚聞神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1936|ja|1936年}}) * {{ill|Fukushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|福島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Fukushima Prefecture Official Ritual Shinhoyama Shrine for the Honoring of Spirits," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Fukushima Inari Shrine|ja|福島稲荷神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})*History of Fukushima City*, vol. 5, Fukushima City Board of Education, 1975, p. 790. * {{ill|Miyazaki Shrine|ja|宮崎神宮}} Museum of Antiquities ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists.*Record of the 2600th Anniversary Commemorative Ceremony*, vol. 7, 1940. * {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) * {{ill|Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine|ja|大宮氷川神社}} administrative office and envoy Saikan ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})Shinohara Naomichi, Watanabe Yōko, "Research on Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine: Sunami Takashi and the Kokui Architecture Research Institute," *Architectural History and Design*, 2016, pp. 359-360, Japanese Architectural Institute, 2016. * {{ill|Akita Gokoku Shrine|ja|秋田県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists. * {{ill|Toyotamahime Shrine (Minamikyūshū)|ja|豊玉姫神社 (南九州市)}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})*Chiran Local History*, Chiran Town, 1962. * {{ill|Inaba Shrine|ja|伊奈波神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}-{{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})*Summary of Inaba Shrine*, Inaba Shrine, 1941. * {{ill|Miyagi Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮城県護国神社}} ({{ill|1942|ja|1942年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid.*History of Miyagi Prefecture*, vol. 13 (Art and Architecture), Miyagi Prefecture History Publication Committee, 1980. * {{ill|Minatogawa Shrine|ja|湊川神社}} Gate ({{ill|1943|ja|1943年}})Kiyozō Kamiyama, "Gate of Minatogawa Shrine," *Travel and Tradition*, vol. 16, issue 7, 1943. * {{ill|Kagoshima Gokoku Shrine|ja|鹿児島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1948|ja|1948年}}) * {{ill|Sōjiji|ja|總持寺}} Great Ancestral Hall (with Tanaka Naitō) ({{ill|1962|ja|1962年}}) * {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社}} First Torii Gate (with Tanaka Naitō) ({{ill|1963|ja|1963年}})Research Records: Memorandum on Investigation into Relics of Former Shugendo Activities in Fukushima Prefecture (3) (http://www.anno-spero.org/renaissance/dentou/history/fukushima_3.html). === Publications === * "Architectural Techniques of Japan" (1937–1938), serialized in ''Tōyō Kenchiku''. * "Restoration of Nikkō Temples" (1957), published in ''Dainikkō''. == References == == Further Reading == * "Nihonmatsu Kōzō and Cultural Merit Award," ''Shūkyō Kōron,'' 1957. * Shinohara Naomichi and Watanabe Yōko, "Research on Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine," ''Architectural History and Design,'' 2016. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} * {{ill|Shrine Construction Division, Jingi-in|ja|神祇院造営課}} * {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} * {{ill|Sunami Takashi|ja|角南隆}} * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:20th-century {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} | practice_name = {{ill|Home Ministry (Japan)|ja|内務省}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|ja|伊奈波神社}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[1884]] [[March 26]] – [[1972]] [[September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[1908]], he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}}. By [[1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[National Treasures of Japan]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺}}. In [[1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}} in [[1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[World War II]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:Nawa Shrine - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|ja|1913年}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * {{ill|Kagoshima Shrine|ja|鹿児島神宮}} main shrine (unknown year) * {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ({{ill|1933|ja|1933年}}).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * {{ill|Nawa Shrine|ja|名和神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Hirakiki Shrine|ja|枚聞神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1936|ja|1936年}}) * {{ill|Fukushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|福島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Fukushima Prefecture Official Ritual Shinhoyama Shrine for the Honoring of Spirits," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Fukushima Inari Shrine|ja|福島稲荷神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})*History of Fukushima City*, vol. 5, Fukushima City Board of Education, 1975, p. 790. * {{ill|Miyazaki Shrine|ja|宮崎神宮}} Museum of Antiquities ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists.*Record of the 2600th Anniversary Commemorative Ceremony*, vol. 7, 1940. * {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) * {{ill|Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine|ja|大宮氷川神社}} administrative office and envoy Saikan ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})Shinohara Naomichi, Watanabe Yōko, "Research on Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine: Sunami Takashi and the Kokui Architecture Research Institute," *Architectural History and Design*, 2016, pp. 359-360, Japanese Architectural Institute, 2016. * {{ill|Akita Gokoku Shrine|ja|秋田県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists. * {{ill|Toyotamahime Shrine (Minamikyūshū)|ja|豊玉姫神社 (南九州市)}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})*Chiran Local History*, Chiran Town, 1962. * {{ill|Inaba Shrine|ja|伊奈波神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}-{{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})*Summary of Inaba Shrine*, Inaba Shrine, 1941. * {{ill|Miyagi Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮城県護国神社}} ({{ill|1942|ja|1942年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid.*History of Miyagi Prefecture*, vol. 13 (Art and Architecture), Miyagi Prefecture History Publication Committee, 1980. * {{ill|Minatogawa Shrine|ja|湊川神社}} Gate ({{ill|1943|ja|1943年}})Kiyozō Kamiyama, "Gate of Minatogawa Shrine," *Travel and Tradition*, vol. 16, issue 7, 1943. * {{ill|Kagoshima Gokoku Shrine|ja|鹿児島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1948|ja|1948年}}) * {{ill|Sōjiji|ja|總持寺}} Great Ancestral Hall (with Tanaka Naitō) ({{ill|1962|ja|1962年}}) * {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社}} First Torii Gate (with Tanaka Naitō) ({{ill|1963|ja|1963年}})Research Records: Memorandum on Investigation into Relics of Former Shugendo Activities in Fukushima Prefecture (3) (http://www.anno-spero.org/renaissance/dentou/history/fukushima_3.html). === Publications === * "Architectural Techniques of Japan" (1937–1938), serialized in ''Tōyō Kenchiku''. * "Restoration of Nikkō Temples" (1957), published in ''Dainikkō''. == References == == Further Reading == * "Nihonmatsu Kōzō and Cultural Merit Award," ''Shūkyō Kōron,'' 1957. * Shinohara Naomichi and Watanabe Yōko, "Research on Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine," ''Architectural History and Design,'' 2016. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} * {{ill|Shrine Construction Division, Jingi-in|ja|神祇院造営課}} * {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} * {{ill|Sunami Takashi|ja|角南隆}} * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:20th-century {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} | practice_name = {{ill|Home Ministry (Japan)|ja|内務省}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|ja|伊奈波神社}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[1884]] [[March 26]] – [[1972]] [[September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[1908]], he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}}. By [[1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[National Treasures of Japan]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺}}. In [[1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}} in [[1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[World War II]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:Nawa Shrine - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|ja|1913年}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * {{ill|Kagoshima Shrine|ja|鹿児島神宮}} main shrine (unknown year) * {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ({{ill|1933|ja|1933年}}).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * {{ill|Nawa Shrine|ja|名和神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Hirakiki Shrine|ja|枚聞神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1936|ja|1936年}}) * {{ill|Fukushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|福島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Fukushima Prefecture Official Ritual Shinhoyama Shrine for the Honoring of Spirits," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Fukushima Inari Shrine|ja|福島稲荷神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})*History of Fukushima City*, vol. 5, Fukushima City Board of Education, 1975, p. 790. * {{ill|Miyazaki Shrine|ja|宮崎神宮}} Museum of Antiquities ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} | practice_name = {{ill|Home Ministry (Japan)|ja|内務省}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|ja|伊奈波神社}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[1884]] [[March 26]] – [[1972]] [[September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[1908]], he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}}. By [[1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[National Treasures of Japan]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺}}. In [[1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}} in [[1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[World War II]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:Nawa Shrine - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|ja|1913年}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * {{ill|Kagoshima Shrine|ja|鹿児島神宮}} main shrine (unknown year) * {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ({{ill|1933|ja|1933年}}).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * {{ill|Nawa Shrine|ja|名和神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Hirakiki Shrine|ja|枚聞神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1936|ja|1936年}}) * {{ill|Fukushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|福島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})*History of Fukushima City*, vol. 5, Fukushima City Board of Education, 1975, p. 790. * {{ill|Miyazaki Shrine|ja|宮崎神宮}} Museum of Antiquities ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} | practice_name = {{ill|Home Ministry (Japan)|ja|内務省}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|ja|伊奈波神社}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[1884]] [[March 26]] – [[1972]] [[September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[1908]], he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}}. By [[1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[National Treasures of Japan]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺}}. In [[1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}} in [[1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[World War II]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:Nawa Shrine - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|ja|1913年}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * {{ill|Kagoshima Shrine|ja|鹿児島神宮}} main shrine (unknown year) * {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ({{ill|1933|ja|1933年}}).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * {{ill|Nawa Shrine|ja|名和神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Hirakiki Shrine|ja|枚聞神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1936|ja|1936年}}) * {{ill|Fukushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|福島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})*History of Fukushima City*, vol. 5, Fukushima City Board of Education, 1975, p. 790. * {{ill|Miyazaki Shrine|ja|宮崎神宮}} Museum of Antiquities ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} | practice_name = {{ill|Home Ministry (Japan)|ja|内務省}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|ja|伊奈波神社}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[1884]] [[March 26]] – [[1972]] [[September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[1908]], he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}}. By [[1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[National Treasures of Japan]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺}}. In [[1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}} in [[1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[World War II]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:Nawa Shrine - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|ja|1913年}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * {{ill|Kagoshima Shrine|ja|鹿児島神宮}} main shrine (unknown year) * {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ({{ill|1933|ja|1933年}}).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * {{ill|Nawa Shrine|ja|名和神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Hirakiki Shrine|ja|枚聞神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1936|ja|1936年}}) * {{ill|Fukushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|福島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})*History of Fukushima City*, vol. 5, Fukushima City Board of Education, 1975, p. 790. * {{ill|Miyazaki Shrine|ja|宮崎神宮}} Museum of Antiquities ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} | practice_name = {{ill|Home Ministry (Japan)|ja|内務省}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|ja|伊奈波神社}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[1884]] [[March 26]] – [[1972]] [[September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[1908]], he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}}. By [[1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[National Treasures of Japan]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺}}. In [[1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}} in [[1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[World War II]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:Nawa Shrine - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|ja|1913年}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * {{ill|Kagoshima Shrine|ja|鹿児島神宮}} main shrine (unknown year) * {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ({{ill|1933|ja|1933年}}).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * {{ill|Nawa Shrine|ja|名和神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Hirakiki Shrine|ja|枚聞神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1936|ja|1936年}}) * {{ill|Fukushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|福島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})*History of Fukushima City*, vol. 5, Fukushima City Board of Education, 1975, p. 790. * {{ill|Miyazaki Shrine|ja|宮崎神宮}} Museum of Antiquities ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} | practice_name = {{ill|Home Ministry (Japan)|ja|内務省}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|ja|伊奈波神社}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[1884]] [[March 26]] – [[1972]] [[September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[1908]], he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}}. By [[1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[National Treasures of Japan]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺}}. In [[1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}} in [[1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[World War II]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:Nawa Shrine - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|ja|1913年}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * {{ill|Kagoshima Shrine|ja|鹿児島神宮}} main shrine (unknown year) * {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ({{ill|1933|ja|1933年}}).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * {{ill|Nawa Shrine|ja|名和神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Hirakiki Shrine|ja|枚聞神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1936|ja|1936年}}) * {{ill|Fukushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|福島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Fukushima Prefecture Official Ritual Shinhoyama Shrine for the Honoring of Spirits," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Fukushima Inari Shrine|ja|福島稲荷神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})*History of Fukushima City*, vol. 5, Fukushima City Board of Education, 1975, p. 790. * {{ill|Miyazaki Shrine|ja|宮崎神宮}} Museum of Antiquities ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists.*Record of the 2600th Anniversary Commemorative Ceremony*, vol. 7, 1940. * {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) * {{ill|Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine|ja|大宮氷川神社}} administrative office and envoy Saikan ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})Shinohara Naomichi, Watanabe Yōko, "Research on Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine: Sunami Takashi and the Kokui Architecture Research Institute," *Architectural History and Design*, 2016, pp. 359-360, Japanese Architectural Institute, 2016. * {{ill|Akita Gokoku Shrine|ja|秋田県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists. * {{ill|Toyotamahime Shrine (Minamikyūshū)|ja|豊玉姫神社 (南九州市)}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})*Chiran Local History*, Chiran Town, 1962. * {{ill|Inaba Shrine|ja|伊奈波神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}-{{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})*Summary of Inaba Shrine*, Inaba Shrine, 1941. * {{ill|Miyagi Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮城県護国神社}} ({{ill|1942|ja|1942年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid.*History of {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} | practice_name = {{ill|Home Ministry (Japan)|ja|内務省}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|ja|伊奈波神社}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[1884]] [[March 26]] – [[1972]] [[September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[1908]], he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}}. By [[1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[National Treasures of Japan]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺}}. In [[1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}} in [[1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[World War II]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:Nawa Shrine - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|ja|1913年}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * {{ill|Kagoshima Shrine|ja|鹿児島神宮}} main shrine (unknown year) * {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ({{ill|1933|ja|1933年}}).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * {{ill|Nawa Shrine|ja|名和神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Hirakiki Shrine|ja|枚聞神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1936|ja|1936年}}) * {{ill|Fukushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|福島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Fukushima Prefecture Official Ritual Shinhoyama Shrine for the Honoring of Spirits," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Fukushima Inari Shrine|ja|福島稲荷神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})*History of Fukushima City*, vol. 5, Fukushima City Board of Education, 1975, p. 790. * {{ill|Miyazaki Shrine|ja|宮崎神宮}} Museum of Antiquities ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists.*Record of the 2600th Anniversary Commemorative Ceremony*, vol. 7, 1940. * {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) * {{ill|Ōmiya Hikawa {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} | practice_name = {{ill|Home Ministry (Japan)|ja|内務省}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|ja|伊奈波神社}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[1884]] [[March 26]] – [[1972]] [[September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[1908]], he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}}. By [[1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[National Treasures of Japan]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺}}. In [[1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}} in [[1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[World War II]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:Nawa Shrine - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|ja|1913年}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * {{ill|Kagoshima Shrine|ja|鹿児島神宮}} main shrine (unknown year) * {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ({{ill|1933|ja|1933年}}).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * {{ill|Nawa Shrine|ja|名和神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Hirakiki Shrine|ja|枚聞神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1936|ja|1936年}}) * {{ill|Fukushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|福島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} | practice_name = {{ill|Home Ministry (Japan)|ja|内務省}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|ja|伊奈波神社}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[1884]] [[March 26]] – [[1972]] [[September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[1908]], he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}}. By [[1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[National Treasures of Japan]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺}}. In [[1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}} in [[1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[World War II]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:Nawa Shrine - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|ja|1913年}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * {{ill|Kagoshima Shrine|ja|鹿児島神宮}} main shrine (unknown year) * {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ({{ill|1933|ja|1933年}}).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * {{ill|Nawa Shrine|ja|名和神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Hirakiki Shrine|ja|枚聞神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1936|ja|1936年}}) * {{ill|Fukushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|福島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Fukushima Prefecture Official Ritual Shinhoyama Shrine for the Honoring of Spirits," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Fukushima Inari Shrine|ja|福島稲荷神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})*History of Fukushima City*, vol. 5, Fukushima City Board of Education, 1975, p. 790. * {{ill|Miyazaki Shrine|ja|宮崎神宮}} Museum of Antiquities ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists.*Record of the 2600th Anniversary Commemorative Ceremony*, vol. 7, 1940. * {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) * {{ill|Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine|ja|大宮氷川神社}} administrative office and envoy Saikan ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})Shinohara Naomichi, Watanabe Yōko, "Research on Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine: Sunami Takashi and the Kokui Architecture Research Institute," *Architectural History and Design*, 2016, pp. 359-360, Japanese Architectural Institute, 2016. * {{ill|Akita Gokoku Shrine|ja|秋田県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists. * {{ill|Toyotamahime Shrine (Minamikyūshū)|ja|豊玉姫神社 (南九州市)}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})*Chiran Local History*, Chiran Town, 1962. * {{ill|Inaba Shrine|ja|伊奈波神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}-{{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})*Summary of Inaba Shrine*, Inaba Shrine, 1941. * {{ill|Miyagi Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮城県護国神社}} ({{ill|1942|ja|1942年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid.*History of Miyagi Prefecture*, vol. 13 (Art and Architecture), Miyagi Prefecture History Publication Committee, 1980. * {{ill|Minatogawa Shrine|ja|湊川神社}} Gate ({{ill|1943|ja|1943年}})Kiyozō Kamiyama, "Gate of Minatogawa Shrine," *Travel and Tradition*, vol. 16, issue 7, 1943. * {{ill|Kagoshima Gokoku Shrine|ja|鹿児島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1948|ja|1948年}}) * {{ill|Sōjiji|ja|總持寺}} Great Ancestral Hall (with Tanaka Naitō) ({{ill|1962|ja|1962年}}) * {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社}} First Torii Gate (with Tanaka Naitō) ({{ill|1963|ja|1963年}})Research Records: Memorandum on Investigation into Relics of Former Shugendo Activities in Fukushima Prefecture (3) (http://www.anno-spero.org/renaissance/dentou/history/fukushima_3.html). === Publications === * "Architectural Techniques of Japan" (1937–1938), serialized in ''Tōyō Kenchiku''. * "Restoration of Nikkō Temples" (1957), published in ''Dainikkō''. == References == == Further Reading == * "Nihonmatsu Kōzō and Cultural Merit Award," ''Shūkyō revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 1]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}} | practice_name = {{ill|Home Ministry (Japan)|ja|内務省}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|ja|伊奈波神社}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[1884]] [[March 26]] – [[1972]] [[September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[1908]], he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|ja|工学院大学}}. By [[1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[National Treasures of Japan]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺}}. In [[1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所}} in [[1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|ja|比叡山}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[World War II]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:Nawa Shrine - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|ja|1913年}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * {{ill|Kagoshima Shrine|ja|鹿児島神宮}} main shrine (unknown year) * {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ({{ill|1933|ja|1933年}}).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1934|ja|1934年}})*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * {{ill|Nawa Shrine|ja|名和神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1935|ja|1935年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Hirakiki Shrine|ja|枚聞神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1936|ja|1936年}}) * {{ill|Fukushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|福島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Fukushima Prefecture Official Ritual Shinhoyama Shrine for the Honoring of Spirits," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Fukushima Inari Shrine|ja|福島稲荷神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1937|ja|1937年}})*History of Fukushima City*, vol. 5, Fukushima City Board of Education, 1975, p. 790. * {{ill|Miyazaki Shrine|ja|宮崎神宮}} Museum of Antiquities ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists.*Record of the 2600th Anniversary Commemorative Ceremony*, vol. 7, 1940. * {{ill|Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) * {{ill|Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine|ja|大宮氷川神社}} administrative office and envoy Saikan ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})Shinohara Naomichi, Watanabe Yōko, "Research on Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine: Sunami Takashi and the Kokui Architecture Research Institute," *Architectural History and Design*, 2016, pp. 359-360, Japanese Architectural Institute, 2016. * {{ill|Akita Gokoku Shrine|ja|秋田県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}}) ※No longer exists. * {{ill|Toyotamahime Shrine (Minamikyūshū)|ja|豊玉姫神社 (南九州市)}} main shrine ({{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})*Chiran Local History*, Chiran Town, 1962. * {{ill|Inaba Shrine|ja|伊奈波神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1932|ja|1932年}}-{{ill|1940|ja|1940年}})*Summary of Inaba Shrine*, Inaba Shrine, 1941. * {{ill|Miyagi Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮城県護国神社}} ({{ill|1942|ja|1942年}}) ※Destroyed in an air raid.*History of Miyagi Prefecture*, vol. 13 (Art and Architecture), Miyagi Prefecture History Publication Committee, 1980. * {{ill|Minatogawa Shrine|ja|湊川神社}} Gate ({{ill|1943|ja|1943年}})Kiyozō Kamiyama, "Gate of Minatogawa Shrine," *Travel and Tradition*, vol. 16, issue 7, 1943. * {{ill|Kagoshima Gokoku Shrine|ja|鹿児島県護国神社}} main shrine ({{ill|1948|ja|1948年}}) * {{ill|Sōjiji|ja|總持寺}} Great Ancestral Hall (with Tanaka Naitō) ({{ill|1962|ja|1962年}}) * {{ill|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine|ja|大洗磯前神社}} First Torii Gate (with Tanaka Naitō) ({{ill|1963|ja|1963年}})Research Records: Memorandum on Investigation into Relics of Former Shugendo Activities in Fukushima Prefecture (3) (http://www.anno-spero.org/renaissance/dentou/history/fukushima_3.html). === Publications === * "Architectural Techniques of Japan" (1937–1938), serialized in ''Tōyō Kenchiku''. * "Restoration of Nikkō Temples" (1957), published in ''Dainikkō''. == References == == Further Reading == * "Nihonmatsu Kōzō and Cultural Merit Award," ''Shūkyō Kōron,'' 1957. * Shinohara Naomichi and Watanabe Yōko, "Research on Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine," ''Architectural History and Design,'' 2016. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}} *
