{{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]] | practice_name = [[:en:Interior ministry|Home Ministry (Japan)]]
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
[[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] | significant_buildings= [[:en:Inaba Shrine|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)]]
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[:en:1884|1884]] [[:en:March 26|March 26]] – [[:en:1972|1972]] [[:en:September 9|September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[:en:1908|1908]], he graduated from what is now [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]]. By [[:en:1911|1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|qq=}}. In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:名和神社 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ([[:en:1913|1913]])Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamauchi Shrine|qq=}} main shrine ([[:en:1932|1932]]) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * [[:en:Kagoshima Shrine|Kagoshima Shrine]] main shrine (unknown year) * [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ([[:en:1933|1933]]).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odo Shrine|qq=}} main shrine ([[:en:1934|1934]])Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Gokoku Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埼玉縣護國神社}} main shrine ([[:en:1934|1934]])*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * [[:en:Nawa Shrine|Nawa Shrine]] main shrine ([[:en:1935|1935]])Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," Official Ritual Shinhoyama Shrine for the Honoring of Spirits," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Fukushima Inari serialized in ''Tōyō Kenchiku''. * "Restoration of Nikkō Temples" (1957), published in ''Dainikkō''. == References == == Further Reading == * "Nihonmatsu Kōzō and Cultural Merit Award," ''Shūkyō Kōron,'' 1957. * Shinohara Naomichi and Watanabe Yōko, "Research on Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine," ''Architectural History and Design,'' 2016. == Related Articles == * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] * {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q124481696]] Wikidata name: Kōzō Nihonmatsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]] | practice_name = [[:en:Interior ministry|Home Ministry (Japan)]]
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
[[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] | significant_buildings= [[:en:Inaba Shrine|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)]]
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[:en:1884|1884]] [[:en:March 26|March 26]] – [[:en:1972|1972]] [[:en:September 9|September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[:en:1908|1908]], he graduated from what is now [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]]. By [[:en:1911|1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|qq=}}. In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:名和神社 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ([[:en:1913|1913]])Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamauchi Shrine|qq=}} main shrine ([[:en:1932|1932]]) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * [[:en:Kagoshima Shrine|Kagoshima Shrine]] main shrine (unknown year) * [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ([[:en:1933|1933]]).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odo Shrine|qq=}} main shrine ([[:en:1934|1934]])Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Gokoku Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埼玉縣護國神社}} main shrine ([[:en:1934|1934]])*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * [[:en:Nawa Shrine|Nawa Shrine]] main shrine ([[:en:1935|1935]])Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," Official Ritual Shinhoyama Shrine for the Honoring of Spirits," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Fukushima Inari Shrine|ja|栃木県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tochigi Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} main shrine ([[:en:1940|1940]]) * {{ill|Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine|ja|大宮氷川神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社}} administrative office and envoy Saikan ([[:en:1940|1940]])Shinohara Naomichi, Watanabe Yōko, "Research on Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine: Sunami Takashi and the Kokui Architecture Research Institute," *Architectural History and Design*, 2016, pp. 359-360, Japanese Architectural Institute, 2016. * {{ill|Akita Gokoku Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮城縣護國神社}} ([[:en:1942|1942]]) ※Destroyed in an air raid.*History of Miyagi Prefecture*, vol. 13 (Art and Architecture), Miyagi Prefecture History Publication Committee, 1980. * [[:en:Minatogawa Shrine|Minatogawa Shrine]] Gate ([[:en:1943|1943]])Kiyozō Kamiyama, "Gate of Minatogawa Shrine," *Travel and Tradition*, vol. 16, issue 7, 1943. * {{ill|Kagoshima ([[:en:1962|1962]]) * [[:en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine]] First Torii Gate (with Tanaka Naitō) ([[:en:1963|1963]])Research Records: Memorandum on Investigation into Relics of Former Shugendo Activities in Fukushima Prefecture (3) (http://www.anno-spero.org/renaissance/dentou/history/fukushima_3.html). === Publications === * "Architectural Techniques of Japan" (1937–1938), serialized in ''Tōyō Kenchiku''. * "Restoration of Nikkō Temples" (1957), published in ''Dainikkō''. == References == == Further Reading == * "Nihonmatsu Kōzō and Cultural Merit Award," ''Shūkyō Kōron,'' 1957. * Shinohara Naomichi and Watanabe Yōko, "Research on Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine," ''Architectural History and Design,'' 2016. == Related Articles == * [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] * In [[:en:1908|1908]], he graduated from what is now [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]]. By [[:en:1911|1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|qq=|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[:en:1884|1884]] [[:en:March 26|March 26]] – [[:en:1972|1972]] [[:en:September 9|September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[:en:1908|1908]], he graduated from what is now [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]]. By [[:en:1911|1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|qq=|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1884|3|26}}|death_date={{death date|1972|9|9}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|architect|qid=Q42973}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q124481696]] Wikidata name: Kōzō Nihonmatsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]] | practice_name = [[:en:Interior ministry|Home Ministry (Japan)]]
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
[[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] | significant_buildings= [[:en:Inaba Shrine|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)]]
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[:en:1884|1884]] [[:en:March 26|March 26]] – [[:en:1972|1972]] [[:en:September 9|September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[:en:1908|1908]], he graduated from what is now [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]]. By [[:en:1911|1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|qq=|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:名和神社 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ([[:en:1913|1913]])Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamauchi Shrine|qq=}} main shrine ([[:en:1932|1932]]) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * [[:en:Kagoshima Shrine|Kagoshima Shrine]] main shrine (unknown year) * [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ([[:en:1933|1933]]).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1884|3|26}}|death_date={{death date|1972|9|9}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|architect|qid=Q42973}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q124481696]] Wikidata name: Kōzō Nihonmatsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]] | practice_name = [[:en:Interior ministry|Home Ministry (Japan)]]
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
[[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] | significant_buildings= [[:en:Inaba Shrine|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)]]
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[:en:1884|1884]] [[:en:March 26|March 26]] – [[:en:1972|1972]] [[:en:September 9|September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[:en:1908|1908]], he graduated from what is now [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]]. By [[:en:1911|1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|qq=|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:名和神社 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ([[:en:1913|1913]])Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamauchi Shrine|qq=}} main shrine ([[:en:1932|1932]]) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * [[:en:Kagoshima Shrine|Kagoshima Shrine]] main shrine (unknown year) * [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ([[:en:1933|1933]]).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odo Shrine|qq=|1=Odo Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460784}} main shrine ([[:en:1934|1934]])Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Gokoku Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埼玉縣護國神社}} main shrine ([[:en:1934|1934]])*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * [[:en:Nawa Shrine|Nawa Shrine]] main shrine ([[:en:1935|1935]])Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * [[:en:Hirasaki Shrine|Hirakiki Shrine]] main shrine ([[:en:1936|1936]]) Shugendo Activities in Fukushima Prefecture (3) (http://www.anno-spero.org/renaissance/dentou/history/fukushima_3.html). === Publications === * "Architectural Techniques of Japan" (1937–1938), serialized in ''Tōyō Kenchiku''. * "Restoration of Nikkō Temples" (1957), published in ''Dainikkō''. == References == == Further Reading == * "Nihonmatsu Kōzō and Cultural Merit Award," ''Shūkyō {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1884|3|26}}|death_date={{death date|1972|9|9}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|architect|qid=Q42973}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q124481696]] Wikidata name: Kōzō Nihonmatsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]] | practice_name = [[:en:Interior ministry|Home Ministry (Japan)]]
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
[[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] | significant_buildings= [[:en:Inaba Shrine|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)]]
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[:en:1884|1884]] [[:en:March 26|March 26]] – [[:en:1972|1972]] [[:en:September 9|September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[:en:1908|1908]], he graduated from what is now [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]]. By [[:en:1911|1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|qq=|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:名和神社 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ([[:en:1913|1913]])Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamauchi Shrine|qq=}} main shrine ([[:en:1932|1932]]) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * [[:en:Kagoshima Shrine|Kagoshima Shrine]] main shrine (unknown year) * [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ([[:en:1933|1933]]).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odo Shrine|qq=|1=Odo Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460784}} main shrine ([[:en:1934|1934]])Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Gokoku Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埼玉縣護國神社}} main shrine ([[:en:1934|1934]])*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * [[:en:Nawa Shrine|Nawa Shrine]] main shrine ([[:en:1935|1935]])Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * [[:en:Hirasaki Shrine|Hirakiki Shrine]] main shrine ([[:en:1936|1936]]) [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1884|3|26}}|death_date={{death date|1972|9|9}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|architect|WD=Q42973}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q124481696]] Wikidata name: Kōzō Nihonmatsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]] | practice_name = [[:en:Interior ministry|Home Ministry (Japan)]]

Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[:en:1884|1884]] [[:en:March 26|March 26]] – [[:en:1972|1972]] [[:en:September 9|September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[:en:1908|1908]], he graduated from what is now [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]]. By [[:en:1911|1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|qq=|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:名和神社 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[:en:1884|1884]] [[:en:March 26|March 26]] – [[:en:1972|1972]] [[:en:September 9|September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[:en:1908|1908]], he graduated from what is now [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]]. By [[:en:1911|1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:名和神社 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ([[:en:1913|1913]])Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamauchi Shrine|qq=}} main shrine ([[:en:1932|1932]]) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * [[:en:Kagoshima Shrine|Kagoshima Shrine]] main shrine (unknown year) * [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ([[:en:1933|1933]]).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odo Shrine|1=Odo Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460784}} main shrine ([[:en:1934|1934]])Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埼玉縣護國神社}} main shrine ([[:en:1934|1934]])*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * [[:en:Nawa Shrine|Nawa Shrine]] main shrine ([[:en:1935|1935]])Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * [[:en:Hirasaki Shrine|Hirakiki Shrine]] main shrine ([[:en:1936|1936]]) [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1884|3|26}}|death_date={{death date|1972|9|9}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|architect|WD=Q42973}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q124481696]] Wikidata name: Kōzō Nihonmatsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]] | practice_name = [[:en:Interior ministry|Home Ministry (Japan)]]
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
[[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] | significant_buildings= [[:en:Inaba Shrine|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)]]
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[:en:1884|1884]] [[:en:March 26|March 26]] – [[:en:1972|1972]] [[:en:September 9|September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[:en:1908|1908]], he graduated from what is now [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]]. By [[:en:1911|1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:名和神社 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ([[:en:1913|1913]])Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * [[:en:Kagoshima Shrine|Kagoshima Shrine]] main shrine (unknown year) * [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ([[:en:1933|1933]]).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1884|3|26}}|death_date={{death date|1972|9|9}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|architect|WD=Q42973}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q124481696]] Wikidata name: Kōzō Nihonmatsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]] | practice_name = [[:en:Interior ministry|Home Ministry (Japan)]]
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
[[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] | significant_buildings= [[:en:Inaba Shrine|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)]]
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[:en:1884|1884]] [[:en:March 26|March 26]] – [[:en:1972|1972]] [[:en:September 9|September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[:en:1908|1908]], he graduated from what is now [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]]. By [[:en:1911|1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:名和神社 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ([[:en:1913|1913]])Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamauchi Shrine|qq=}} main shrine ([[:en:1932|1932]]) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * [[:en:Kagoshima Shrine|Kagoshima Shrine]] main shrine (unknown year) * [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ([[:en:1933|1933]]).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1884|3|26}}|death_date={{death date|1972|9|9}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|architect|WD=Q42973}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q124481696]] Wikidata name: Kōzō Nihonmatsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]] | practice_name = [[:en:Interior ministry|Home Ministry (Japan)]]
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[:en:1884|1884]] [[:en:March 26|March 26]] – [[:en:1972|1972]] [[:en:September 9|September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[:en:1908|1908]], he graduated from what is now [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]]. By [[:en:1911|1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == 二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]] | practice_name = [[:en:Interior ministry|Home Ministry (Japan)]]
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
[[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] | significant_buildings= [[:en:Inaba Shrine|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)]]
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[:en:1884|1884]] [[:en:March 26|March 26]] – [[:en:1972|1972]] [[:en:September 9|September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[:en:1908|1908]], he graduated from what is now [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]]. By [[:en:1911|1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:名和神社 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ([[:en:1913|1913]])Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamauchi Shrine|qq=}} main shrine ([[:en:1932|1932]]) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * [[:en:Kagoshima Shrine|Kagoshima Shrine]] main shrine (unknown year) * [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ([[:en:1933|1933]]).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Odo Shrine|ja|小戸神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odo Shrine|1=Odo Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11460784}} main shrine ([[:en:1934|1934]])Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Renovation Plans for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埼玉縣護國神社}} main shrine ([[:en:1934|1934]])*Saitama Prefecture History*, vol. 7, Saitama Prefecture, 1939. * [[:en:Nawa Shrine|Nawa Shrine]] main shrine ([[:en:1935|1935]])Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Main Shrine of Nawa Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 4, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * [[:en:Hirasaki Shrine|Hirakiki Shrine]] main shrine ([[:en:1936|1936]]) * {{ill|Fukushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|福島県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushima Gokoku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 福島縣護國神社}} main shrine ([[:en:1937|1937]])Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Fukushima Prefecture Official Ritual Shinhoyama Shrine for the Honoring of Spirits," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Fukushima Inari Shrine|ja|福島稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushima Inari Shrine|1=Fukushima Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11593701}} main shrine ([[:en:1937|1937]])*History of Fukushima City*, vol. 5, Fukushima City Board of Education, 1975, p. 790. * [[:en:Miyazaki Jingū|Miyazaki Shrine]] Museum of Antiquities ([[:en:1940|1940]]) ※No longer exists.*Record of the 2600th Anniversary Commemorative Ceremony*, vol. 7, 1940. * {{ill|Tochigi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社|1=Hikawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q703633}} administrative office and envoy Saikan ([[:en:1940|1940]])Shinohara Naomichi, Watanabe Yōko, "Research on Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine: Sunami Takashi and the Kokui Architecture Research Institute," *Architectural History and Design*, 2016, pp. 359-360, Japanese Architectural Institute, 2016. * {{ill|Akita Gokoku Shrine|ja|秋田県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} main shrine ([[:en:1940|1940]]) ※No longer exists. * {{ill|Toyotamahime Shrine (Minamikyūshū)|ja|豊玉姫神社 History*, Chiran Town, 1962. * [[:en:Inaba Shrine|Inaba Shrine]] main shrine ([[:en:1932|1932]]-[[:en:1940|1940]])*Summary of Inaba Shrine*, Inaba Shrine, 1941. * {{ill|Miyagi Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮城県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮城縣護國神社}} ([[:en:1942|1942]]) ※Destroyed in an air raid.*History of Miyagi Prefecture*, vol. 13 (Art and Architecture), Miyagi Prefecture History Publication Committee, 1980. * [[:en:Minatogawa Shrine|Minatogawa Shrine]] Gate ([[:en:1943|1943]])Kiyozō Kamiyama, "Gate of Minatogawa Shrine," *Travel and Tradition*, vol. 16, issue 7, 1943. * {{ill|Kagoshima Gokoku Shrine|ja|鹿児島県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagoshima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} main shrine ([[:en:1948|1948]]) * [[:en:Sōji-ji|Sōjiji]] Great Ancestral Hall (with Tanaka Naitō) ([[:en:1962|1962]]) * [[:en:Oarai Isosaki Shrine|Ōarai Isosaki Shrine]] First Torii Gate (with Tanaka Naitō) ([[:en:1963|1963]])Research Records: Memorandum on Investigation into Relics of Former Shugendo Activities in Fukushima Prefecture (3) (http://www.anno-spero.org/renaissance/dentou/history/fukushima_3.html). === Publications === * "Architectural Techniques of Japan" (1937–1938), serialized in ''Tōyō Kenchiku''. * "Restoration of Nikkō Temples" (1957), published in ''Dainikkō''. == References == == Further Reading == * "Nihonmatsu Kōzō and Cultural Merit Award," ''Shūkyō Kōron,'' 1957. * Shinohara Naomichi and Watanabe Yōko, "Research on Ōmiya Hikawa Shrine," ''Architectural History and Design,'' 2016. == Related Articles == * In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[:en:1884|1884]] [[:en:March 26|March 26]] – [[:en:1972|1972]] [[:en:September 9|September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[:en:1908|1908]], he graduated from what is now [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]]. By [[:en:1911|1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1884|3|26}}|death_date={{death date|1972|9|9}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|architect|WD=Q42973}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q124481696]] Wikidata name: Kōzō Nihonmatsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]] | practice_name = [[:en:Interior ministry|Home Ministry (Japan)]]
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
[[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] | significant_buildings= [[:en:Inaba Shrine|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)]]
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[:en:1884|1884]] [[:en:March 26|March 26]] – [[:en:1972|1972]] [[:en:September 9|September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[:en:1908|1908]], he graduated from what is now [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]]. By [[:en:1911|1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1884|3|26}}|death_date={{death date|1972|9|9}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|architect|WD=Q42973}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q124481696]] Wikidata name: Kōzō Nihonmatsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]] | practice_name = [[:en:Interior ministry|Home Ministry (Japan)]]
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
[[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] | significant_buildings= [[:en:Inaba Shrine|Inaba Shrine (Gifu)]]
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, [[:en:1884|1884]] [[:en:March 26|March 26]] – [[:en:1972|1972]] [[:en:September 9|September 9]]) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]]''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In [[:en:1908|1908]], he graduated from what is now [[:en:Kogakuin University|Kogakuin University]]. By [[:en:1911|1911]], he was supervising the repair of [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure (Japan)]] such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In [[:en:1917|1917]], he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:1927|1927]]''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]] during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In [[:en:1958|1958]], he received the Cultural Merit Award from [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], and in [[:en:1966|1966]] he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:名和神社 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ([[:en:1913|1913]])Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamauchi Shrine|qq=}} main shrine ([[:en:1932|1932]]) ※Destroyed in an air raid."Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine Dedicated to Yamauchi Yūdo and Yamauchi Toyonori," Yamauchi Shrine Kiseikai, 1935. * [[:en:Kagoshima Shrine|Kagoshima Shrine]] main shrine (unknown year) * [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] envoy hall, Saikan (purification hall), and administrative office ([[:en:1933|1933]]).Kōzō Nihonmatsu, "Construction of Izumo Taisha Envoy Hall, Saikan, and Administrative Office," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 2, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * for Odo Shrine," *Eastern Architecture*, vol. 1, issue 1, Sagami Shobō, 1937. * {{ill|Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja|埼玉県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitama Gokoku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1884|3|26}}|death_date={{death date|1972|9|9}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|architect|WD=Q42973}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q124481696]] Wikidata name: Kōzō Nihonmatsu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}} | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|en|Kogakuin University|WD=Q509250|lt=Kogakuin University}} | practice_name = {{ill|Interior ministry|en|Interior ministry|WD=Q6589202|lt=Home Ministry (Japan)}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine|en|Inaba Shrine|WD=Q701414|lt=Inaba Shrine (Gifu)}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, {{ill|1884|en|1884|WD=Q7819|lt=1884}} {{ill|March 26|en|March 26|WD=Q2456|lt=March 26}} – {{ill|1972|en|1972|WD=Q2476|lt=1972}} {{ill|September 9|en|September 9|WD=Q2856|lt=September 9}}) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}}''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In {{ill|1908|en|1908|WD=Q2056|lt=1908}}, he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|en|Kogakuin University|WD=Q509250|lt=Kogakuin University}}. By {{ill|1911|en|1911|WD=Q2076|lt=1911}}, he was supervising the repair of {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)}} such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In {{ill|1917|en|1917|WD=Q2092|lt=1917}}, he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|1927|en|1927|WD=Q18110|lt=1927}}''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|WD=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In {{ill|1958|en|1958|WD=Q5253|lt=1958}}, he received the Cultural Merit Award from {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|en|Tochigi Prefecture|WD=Q44843|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}}, and in {{ill|1966|en|1966|WD=Q2649|lt=1966}} he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:名和神社 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|en|1913|WD=Q2080|lt=1913}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamauchi Shrine|qq=}} main shrine By {{ill|1911|en|1911|WD=Q2076|lt=1911}}, he was supervising the repair of {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)}} such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|WD=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1884|3|26}}|death_date={{death date|1972|9|9}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|architect|WD=Q42973}}}} {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}} | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|en|Kogakuin University|WD=Q509250|lt=Kogakuin University}} | practice_name = {{ill|Interior ministry|en|Interior ministry|WD=Q6589202|lt=Home Ministry (Japan)}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine|en|Inaba Shrine|WD=Q701414|lt=Inaba Shrine (Gifu)}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, {{ill|1884|en|1884|WD=Q7819|lt=1884}} {{ill|March 26|en|March 26|WD=Q2456|lt=March 26}} – {{ill|1972|en|1972|WD=Q2476|lt=1972}} {{ill|September 9|en|September 9|WD=Q2856|lt=September 9}}) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}}''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In {{ill|1908|en|1908|WD=Q2056|lt=1908}}, he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|en|Kogakuin University|WD=Q509250|lt=Kogakuin University}}. By {{ill|1911|en|1911|WD=Q2076|lt=1911}}, he was supervising the repair of {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)}} such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In {{ill|1917|en|1917|WD=Q2092|lt=1917}}, he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|1927|en|1927|WD=Q18110|lt=1927}}''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|WD=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In {{ill|1958|en|1958|WD=Q5253|lt=1958}}, he received the Cultural Merit Award from {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|en|Tochigi Prefecture|WD=Q44843|lt=Tochigi Prefecture}}, and in {{ill|1966|en|1966|WD=Q2649|lt=1966}} he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:名和神社 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|en|1913|WD=Q2080|lt=1913}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamauchi Shrine|qq=}} main shrine
