[[ja:二本松孝蔵]] [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1884|3|26}}|death_date={{death date|1972|9|9}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|1=Empire of Japan}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|architect|WD=Q42973|1=Architect}}}} {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|en|Kogakuin University|WD=Q509250|lt=Kogakuin University|1=Kogakuin University}} | practice_name = {{ill|Interior ministry|en|Interior ministry|WD=Q6589202|lt=Home Ministry (Japan)|1=Interior ministry}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine|en|Inaba Shrine|WD=Q701414|lt=Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|1=Inaba Shrine}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, {{ill|1884|en|1884|WD=Q7819|lt=1884|1=1884}} {{ill|March 26|en|March 26|WD=Q2456|lt=March 26|1=March 26}} – {{ill|1972|en|1972|WD=Q2476|lt=1972|1=1972}} {{ill|September 9|en|September 9|WD=Q2856|lt=September 9|1=September 9}}) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In {{ill|1908|en|1908|WD=Q2056|lt=1908|1=1908}}, he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|en|Kogakuin University|WD=Q509250|lt=Kogakuin University|1=Kogakuin University}}. By {{ill|1911|en|1911|WD=Q2076|lt=1911|1=1911}}, he was supervising the repair of {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)|1=National Treasure (Japan)}} such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In {{ill|1917|en|1917|WD=Q2092|lt=1917|1=1917}}, he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|1927|en|1927|WD=Q18110|lt=1927|1=1927}}''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|WD=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In {{ill|1958|en|1958|WD=Q5253|lt=1958|1=1958}}, he received the Cultural Merit Award from {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|en|Tochigi Prefecture|WD=Q44843|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|1=Tochigi Prefecture}}, and in {{ill|1966|en|1966|WD=Q2649|lt=1966|1=1966}} he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:名和神社 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[File:Toyotamahime Shrine honden.JPG|thumb|200px|Toyotamahime Shrine]] [[File:Inaba-jinja Gifu Japan06-rjpg.jpg|thumb|200px|Inaba Shrine]] [[File:Minatogawa Shrine Gate.jpg|thumb|200px|Minatogawa Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|en|1913|WD=Q2080|lt=1913|1=1913}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family Residence,'" Japanese Architectural Institute Tohoku Branch Research Report Series, Planning, June 2016. * {{ill|Yamauchi Shrine|ja|山内神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamauchi Shrine|qq=}} main shrine Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, {{ill|1884|en|1884|WD=Q7819|lt=1884|1=1884}} {{ill|March 26|en|March 26|WD=Q2456|lt=March 26|1=March 26}} – {{ill|1972|en|1972|WD=Q2476|lt=1972|1=1972}} {{ill|September 9|en|September 9|WD=Q2856|lt=September 9|1=September 9}}) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In {{ill|1908|en|1908|WD=Q2056|lt=1908|1=1908}}, he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|en|Kogakuin University|WD=Q509250|lt=Kogakuin University|1=Kogakuin University}}. By {{ill|1911|en|1911|WD=Q2076|lt=1911|1=1911}}, he was supervising the repair of {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)|1=National Treasure (Japan)}} such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486487}}. In {{ill|1917|en|1917|WD=Q2092|lt=1917|1=1917}}, he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|1927|en|1927|WD=Q18110|lt=1927|1=1927}}''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|WD=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In {{ill|1958|en|1958|WD=Q5253|lt=1958|1=1958}}, he received the Cultural Merit Award from {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|en|Tochigi Prefecture|WD=Q44843|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|1=Tochigi Prefecture}}, and in {{ill|1966|en|1966|WD=Q2649|lt=1966|1=1966}} he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:名和神社 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Nawa Shrine]] [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] [[ja:二本松孝蔵]] {{Infobox person|birth_date={{birth date|1884|3|26}}|death_date={{death date|1972|9|9}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|1=Empire of Japan}}; {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|architect|qid=Q42973|1=Architect}}}} {{Infobox architect | image = File:二本松孝蔵.png | image_size = 200px | caption = From ''Record of the Reconstruction of Yamauchi Shrine'' | name = Nihonmatsu Kōzō
二本松 孝蔵 | nationality = {{JPN}} | birth_date = {{Birth date|1884|3|26}} | birth_place = {{ill|Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja|小高町 (福島県相馬郡)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odaka Town, Soma District, Fukushima|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|qid=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} | death_date = {{Death date and age|1972|9|9|1884|3|26}} | death_place = Tokyo | alma_mater = {{ill|Kogakuin University|en|Kogakuin University|qid=Q509250|lt=Kogakuin University|1=Kogakuin University}} | practice_name = {{ill|Interior ministry|en|Interior ministry|qid=Q6589202|lt=Home Ministry (Japan)|1=Interior ministry}}
{{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qid=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} | significant_buildings= {{ill|Inaba Shrine|en|Inaba Shrine|qid=Q701414|lt=Inaba Shrine (Gifu)|1=Inaba Shrine}}
Others | awards = Order of the Sacred Treasure, 4th Class }} '''Nihonmatsu Kōzō''' (二本松 孝蔵, {{ill|1884|en|1884|qid=Q7819|lt=1884|1=1884}} {{ill|March 26|en|March 26|qid=Q2456|lt=March 26|1=March 26}} – {{ill|1972|en|1972|qid=Q2476|lt=1972|1=1972}} {{ill|September 9|en|September 9|qid=Q2856|lt=September 9|1=September 9}}) was a Japanese architect and conservation specialist for cultural properties. He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|qid=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In {{ill|1908|en|1908|qid=Q2056|lt=1908|1=1908}}, he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|en|Kogakuin University|qid=Q509250|lt=Kogakuin University|1=Kogakuin University}}. By {{ill|1911|en|1911|qid=Q2076|lt=1911|1=1911}}, he was supervising the repair of {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)|1=National Treasure (Japan)}} such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11486487}}. In {{ill|1917|en|1917|qid=Q2092|lt=1917|1=1917}}, he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|1927|en|1927|qid=Q18110|lt=1927|1=1927}}''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|qid=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qid=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414.. In {{ill|1958|en|1958|qid=Q5253|lt=1958|1=1958}}, he received the Cultural Merit Award from {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|en|Tochigi Prefecture|qid=Q44843|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|1=Tochigi Prefecture}}, and in {{ill|1966|en|1966|qid=Q2649|lt=1966|1=1966}} he was awarded the Fourth Order of the Sacred Treasure. == Notable Works == === Designs === [[File:名和神社 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Nawa Shrine]] Shrine Gate]] * Amano Family Residence, Odaka Ward ({{ill|1913|en|1913|qid=Q2080|lt=1913|1=1913}})Hayami Kiyotaka, Kano Katsushige, "The Career of Architect Kōzō Nihonmatsu and 'Amano Family He is renowned for his preservation, restoration, and design of numerous shrines and temples. == Biography == Nihonmatsu was born in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|qid=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|1=Fukushima Prefecture}}''Taishū Jinjiroku'', 1940 Edition, p. 570.. In {{ill|1908|en|1908|qid=Q2056|lt=1908|1=1908}}, he graduated from what is now {{ill|Kogakuin University|en|Kogakuin University|qid=Q509250|lt=Kogakuin University|1=Kogakuin University}}. By {{ill|1911|en|1911|qid=Q2076|lt=1911|1=1911}}, he was supervising the repair of {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)|1=National Treasure (Japan)}} such as the restoration of Jizō Hall at {{ill|Enmei-ji|ja|延命寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enmei-ji|1=Enmei-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11486487}}. In {{ill|1917|en|1917|qid=Q2092|lt=1917|1=1917}}, he became a technical officer for shrine construction, later founding the {{ill|Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja|国粋建築研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokusui Architecture Research Institute|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|1927|en|1927|qid=Q18110|lt=1927|1=1927}}''Kensetsukai Shinsuroku Kantō-hen,'' 1962, p. 769.. His designs for shrines and temples include projects like the [[Minobu-san]] Mausoleum and extensive repair work at {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|qid=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}}''Shūkyō Kōron,'' Vol. 27, No. 10, 1957, p. 10.. After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, Nihonmatsu continued as a conservation expert, leading major restoration efforts for {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qid=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}} during the Shōwa period. He renamed his office the Shrine and Temple Architecture Research Institute''Nihon Jōkunsya Meikan,'' 1978, p. 1414..
